The boys are back! Burke, James and Pierce are live in 10 minutes with a walkthrough of the latest enhancements to VS Code's Agent Host Protocol! This is a fascinating Rorschach test.

Codex better at backend, security, many general coding tasks.

Claude better at project management, communication.

You can tell how someone thinks, what they’re working on, and what their values are by what they prefer.
I keep hitting quota limits from GitHub's API. This hasn't been designed with agents in mind.

Is this a system prompt or a user prompt? I would not be relying on a user level prompt for protecting against abuse.

I notice there is an instruction to Claude in the comment. It seems risky to teach Claude to follow instructions it finds in source code comments.
Gemini has been posting its solutions directly to https://t.co/fqfl9BoXzj. Everyone is still in the "open due to lack of attention" stage of math discovery.

It’s nice gig making LLMs.

You can distribute free models that suck.

Hype them to the skies.

Take credit for the hundreds of millions of user you’ve got.

And when they make a mistake, you can blame the users for not using the paid models.

Tip: Figure out your personal ceiling for running multiple agents in parallel.

We need to accept that more agents running doesn't mean more of _you_ available. The narrative is still mostly about throughput and parallelism, but almost nobody's talking about what it actually

Agreed; timing will make a huge difference.

Which is *why* honest examination of technical strengths is so essential.

People are too obsessed with benchmarks for open models. The core determining factor of success often is:
1. Immediate & long term tooling support.
2. Finetunability

Tbh Gemma has struggled here in the past. Qwen has excelled at it. It's where the winners are crowned. https://t.co/LkGb3tHjA3

I tried an exercise yesterday. After Gemma4 release, I asked both Codex and Claude Code to implement the model (from the as yet unmerged JAX impl) in clean, canonical PyTorch. They both dutifully reproduced working text decoders within a few hours that remapped and loaded

here's a fantastic visual guide to how gemma 4 works!

also featuring a quote i deeply identify with:

"Developing incredible research and sharing it with the world is something that even to this day, is ingrained in the culture of [@GoogleDeepMind] research teams."

❤️🧑‍🔬

It’s 11th year and counting! Teaching the first lecture of @cs231n every year has been a highlight of my spring seasons. As usual, I asked students which departments or schools they come from @Stanford . Increasingly, students raise their hands to indicate that they come from all https://t.co/XZNGOYq9Ue

Thanks Codex, inspires much confidence...

  So the current evidence is:

  - preprocessing: basically right
  - vision tower: basically right
  - image injection / expanded token semantics: not right enough yet

pretraining research, with its kernels and optimizers, is often perceived as more "cracked" than humanistic vibes-driven posttraining research

but due to weaker verifiability, the latter seems much further away from being automated

plan your career accordingly

Fortunately, I have the opposite problem: the more things Codex builds, the more ideas I get to create! It’s like living in the ADHD heaven 😅

Google dropped 4 different Gemma open-weight models! I'm most excited that they're finally adopting a standard Apache 2.0 open source license. This'll massively boost adoption. The standard of better licenses was set by mostly Chinese open model labs, and now labs in the U.S. https://t.co/c3oRipJgKr

people don’t understand the work ethic required to be a professional researcher. a pro basketball player’s job is to make baskets so they shoot 500 shots a day. my job is to have ideas so I take 14 showers
